I'm not talking about "abortion rights."  I'm talking about a woman's right to control her own body. Actually, anyone's right to control their own body. When reduced to it's simplest components, it is just that, simple. Just like any other Constitutional right or combination of rights, when you get right down to it, its simple. From there it can (and usually does) get more complicated.  Like Freedom of Speech. You either have it as an inalienable right, or you don't. Are there some limitations, as in cases of overlapping right? Yes. For instance, you can't shout "fire in a crowded theater" when there is no fire and your principal purpose is to create false, dangerous and unnecessary panic.  But you can shout fire in a crowded theater if that is true, even though doing so is dangerous. That is the difference between protected and unprotected speech.  I value my constitutional rights enough to die for them, and many, many  people have died in that service. Freedom is not free.

Well, the historical picture is less clear, Gary.  There certainly is much evidence that women deacons existed in the early centuries of both the Eastern and Western Churches.  But there is considerable disagreement about if they were ordained or appointed, and if ordained, just what their functions as ministers were (as opposed to male deacons).  In the Western Church, Women deacons, too, preached the Gospel but only to other women, and went into places where men were not allowed.  The exact history of female deacons - particularly in Roman Catholicism - in muddied with twists and turns, regional and cultural differences, thus the varied interpretations.  By the twelfth century, women deacons disappeared in both Eastern and Western churches, it seems that menstruation was the reason for forbidding women being ordained to any clerical rank.  We need to see what the commission of the female diaconate comes up with and start a new conversation from there.

How long do we have until the next slowdown?  And how severe will that slowdown be?
